title: Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’
summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of University of Michigan CME item CME00092, a keyed encoding of Hermann Varnhagen's complete four-page 1881 Anglia note. Varnhagen directly transcribed the incomplete 74-line Auchinleck fragment of The Thrush and the Nightingale from NLS Adv. MS 19.2.1, f. 279va–vb. The earlier, complete 192-line version in Bodleian MS Digby 86 is a parallel witness discussed through Wright and Halliwell, Stengel, and Hazlitt, not Varnhagen's direct source for the encoded fragment.
review_notes: The lineage separates the abstract poem, its two witnesses, nineteenth-century editions and cataloging, modern digital encodings, and image surrogates.

The Auchinleck cache is complete for the 694 image surfaces published by the provider's facsimile project. It aggregates the main NLS codex and detached leaves held at three other institutions and does not include separate covers, boards, spine, pastedowns, or binding views; it must not be described as one complete current physical codex.

Public web access and private research caching do not establish permission to redistribute source files. NLS project terms expressly prohibit unauthorized commercial use or publication; Bodleian images use CC BY-NC 4.0.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00092
entities.description: A publicly accessible keyed and structurally marked-up electronic text of Varnhagen's article.

entities.label: Varnhagen 1881 article and Auchinleck-fragment edition
entities.description: The complete article represented by CME00092: a German preface followed by a direct transcription of the incomplete Auchinleck fragment and two textual notes.

bibliographic.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, ‘XIII. Zu dem “Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall”,’ Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 4 (1881): 207–210.
bibliographic.title: XIII. Zu dem ‘Streitgedichte zwischen Drossel und Nachtigall’

notes: Printed p. 211 begins an unrelated article on Hamlet, confirming that pp. 207–210 are the complete item.
Varnhagen signs the note at Greifswald; this is an author dateline, not evidence that the medieval poem was composed there.

entities.label: The Thrush and the Nightingale
alternate_labels: Summer is comen with love to toun
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English bird debate as an abstract work, distinct from both surviving witnesses and all modern editions. DIMEV describes twelve-line stanzas with the rhyme pattern aabccbddbeeb.
notes: The Digby witness is complete at 192 lines; the Auchinleck witness preserves only 74 lines and begins and ends imperfectly.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.2.1 (Auchinleck Manuscript)
entities.description: The direct manuscript source of Varnhagen's encoded fragment. The Thrush and the Nightingale occupies both columns of f. 279v and is incomplete at both beginning and end.

holding.notes: The principal codex is at NLS; ten leaves detached from the manuscript are now held in Edinburgh, St Andrews, and London collections and are represented separately within the NLS facsimile project.

physical_description.extent: 331 folios plus 14 stubs in the principal codex; ten further detached folios identified by the project
notes: The project describes 47 surviving quires organized into twelve fascicles.
The present binding is later; losses include leaves and miniatures.
The manuscript was produced in London. That witness-production place is not a secure place of composition for the poem.
The current NLS catalog route was access-challenged in this environment; the official Auchinleck project pages supplied the directly verified physical and textual evidence.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Digby 86
entities.description: A multilingual Worcestershire miscellany containing the complete 192-line witness of The Thrush and the Nightingale at ff. 136vb–138rb.

physical_description.format: Religious, medical, and secular verse and prose miscellany
notes: Languages represented in the catalog metadata include Latin, Middle English, and Old French.
The rubric at the opening on f. 136vb reads ‘Ci comence le cuntent par entre le Mauuis et la russinole.’
This is a parallel witness and the source of the complete nineteenth-century editions discussed by Varnhagen, not the manuscript he says he transcribed for CME00092.

entities.label: NLS Auchinleck Manuscript digital facsimile project
entities.description: The provider-published image project for the principal Auchinleck codex and ten known detached leaves, accompanied by complete transcriptions and editorial material.
entities.reproduction_format: 694 provider-published IIIF image surfaces exposed through item pages and the NLS image service
notes: Project-level completeness is not physical-object completeness: the inventory aggregates separate current holdings and does not expose separate exterior or binding views.
The target folio is image 566 of 694, label 279v.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of MS Digby 86
entities.description: A 430-canvas IIIF Presentation 2 sequence from upper cover through lower cover.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and JPEG image service
notes: The poem occupies image/canvas indices 280–283: fols. 136v, 137r, 137v, and 138r.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, eds., Reliquiae Antiquae: Scraps from Ancient Manuscripts, Illustrating Chiefly Early English Literature and the English Language, vol. 1 (London: John Russell Smith, 1845), 241–245.
bibliographic.title: Reliquiae Antiquae

bibliographic.citation: David Laing, ed., A Penni Worth of Witte: Florice and Blauncheflour, and Other Pieces of Ancient English Poetry (Edinburgh: Abbotsford Club, 1857), 45–48.
bibliographic.title: A Penni Worth of Witte: Florice and Blauncheflour, and Other Pieces of Ancient English Poetry

entities.label: Hazlitt 1864 Digby edition

bibliographic.citation: W. Carew Hazlitt, ed., Remains of the Early Popular Poetry of England, vol. 1 (London: John Russell Smith, 1864), 50–57.
bibliographic.title: Remains of the Early Popular Poetry of England

notes: Hazlitt says the poems are printed after renewed recourse to the manuscripts; Varnhagen nevertheless observes that several errors from the earlier print were carried over.

bibliographic.title: Codicem manu scriptum Digby 86, in Bibliotheca Bodleiana asservatum, descripsit, excerpsit, illustravit

editorial_practices.source_feature: The Auchinleck witness is one incomplete 74-line version of a poem also surviving complete in Digby 86.
editorial_practices.representation: Varnhagen prints the entire surviving Auchinleck fragment and frames it with a short comparison of prior editions.
editorial_practices.description: The article is complete, but the medieval text it contains is a complete transcription of an incomplete witness, not a complete edition of the abstract poem.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Auchinleck fragment and readings from the Digby witness
editorial_practices.representation: Two notes compare readings and sense, one explicitly reporting a Digby reading as ‘D hat’.
editorial_practices.description: Varnhagen directly transcribes Auchinleck and uses Digby readings for comparison; the article does not establish that he directly retranscribed the complete Digby witness.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Italicized letters and words in Varnhagen's transcription
editorial_practices.representation: The CME XML preserves italic spans as HI REND='italic'.
editorial_practices.description: Italics are retained as source typography. Their exact correspondence to abbreviation signs on f. 279v remains to be checked sign by sign; italics must not automatically be relabeled as manuscript text or modern silent expansion.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Verse lines, five speaker headings, and two dotted terminal lacuna placeholders
editorial_practices.representation: All 81 display rows are L elements inside seven LG wrappers.
editorial_practices.description: The XML structure preserves display grouping but does not imply 81 medieval verse lines or seven complete twelve-line stanzas. The audited verse count is 74.

evidence.citation: CME00092 XML in the pinned CME submodule, structurally inspected 2026-07-12.
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete article boundary, German preface, poem text, printed and manuscript milestones, notes, signature, and the audited distinction between 74 verse lines and seven non-verse display L elements.

evidence.locator: printed pp. 207–210; PDF pp. 219–222; next article begins printed p. 211/PDF p. 223
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete article, direct-transcription statement, historical source citations, two comparative notes, and article end.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 5052 (IMEV/NIMEV 3222), witness and bibliography records.

evidence.summary: Supplies title, form, rhyme pattern, subjects, Digby locus and 192-line extent, Auchinleck locus and 74-line imperfect extent, manuscript rubric, and edition cross-references.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript digital edition, ‘The Thrush and the Nightingale.’
evidence.summary: Provides the current official transcription and page-image route and confirms the 74-line incomplete fragment on both columns of f. 279v.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript home, physical make-up, and project pages.
evidence.summary: Identifies the manuscript, 1331–1340 London production, current extent and detached leaves, and the project's full-transcription and digital-facsimile scope.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript, ‘How to refer to this site.’
evidence.summary: States that site texts, essays, images, descriptions, and reference materials are copyrighted, permits personal use by students, scholars, and the public, and prohibits unauthorized commercial use or publication.

evidence.citation: Inventory and integrity audit of all unique official Auchinleck image links exposed across the project's 44 text pages.
evidence.locator: 694 inventory entries; first 001r; target 279v at index 566; last sta_4; source-list.json and inventory.json
evidence.summary: Confirms 694 successful JPEGs, ZIP integrity, target mapping, sample image signatures and dimensions, inclusion of detached-fragment identifiers, and absence of separately published exterior/binding views in the discovered project inventory.
notes: Sample dimensions were 1600×2085 for 001r, 1600×2145 for target 279v, and 1601×452 for detached-fragment image sta_4.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Digby 86 Digital Bodleian object and IIIF Presentation 2 manifest.
evidence.summary: Supplies shelfmark, multilingual miscellany description, 1272–1282 Worcestershire production, 430-canvas sequence, image attribution, and CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

evidence.citation: Integrity and inventory audit of the complete MS Digby 86 IIIF bundle.
evidence.locator: 430 canvases; Upper cover to Lower cover; target images 280–283
evidence.summary: Confirms complete manifest capture, ZIP integrity, and the exact canvas/image mapping for fols. 136v–138r.

evidence.citation: Direct visual inspection of Digital Bodleian images for MS Digby 86, fols. 136v and 138r.
evidence.summary: Visually confirms the red opening rubric and incipit in the right column of f. 136v and inspection of the terminal f. 138r surface with adjacent following content; DIMEV's exact locus ff. 136vb–138rb is retained.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, eds., Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. 1 (1845), 241–245; complete-volume scan and scandata.
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete Digby edition, source heading, rubric, and five-page extent cited by Varnhagen.

evidence.citation: David Laing, ed., A Penni Worth of Witte (1857), 45–48; complete-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms Laing's earlier four-page printing of the incomplete Auchinleck fragment explicitly cited by Varnhagen.

evidence.citation: W. Carew Hazlitt, ed., Remains of the Early Popular Poetry of England, vol. 1 (1864), 50–57; complete-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms the Digby-based edition and its statement that the manuscripts were consulted again; Varnhagen's critique of inherited earlier errors is preserved separately.

evidence.citation: Edmund Stengel, Codicem manu scriptum Digby 86 (1871), 64–65; complete-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms Stengel's Digby entry, rubric and locus, Wright/Halliwell reference, and the collation beginning on p. 64 that Varnhagen cites.

open_questions.question: Will NLS and the other image-rights holders authorize public repository redistribution or commercial/print-on-demand use of any Auchinleck project images or transcriptions?
next_steps: Request written permission from NLS describing the exact repository, public-download, self-publishing, and commercial possibilities.
Do not stage or publish the cached image bundle unless the authorization covers the intended use and all contributing institutions.
notes: The medieval wording may be used independently as public-domain source material, but the provider's modern images and transcription remain separate protected components.

open_questions.question: Which italic spans in Varnhagen and CME are manuscript abbreviation expansions, supplied restorations, or other editorial emphasis?
next_steps: Audit each italic span against the f. 279v image at the highest resolution allowed for research.
Record expansion marks and supplied letters separately rather than inferring a single meaning from italics.

open_questions.question: Which modern critical edition best establishes the relationship between the Digby and Auchinleck versions and supplies current dating or localization arguments?
next_steps: Review the later editions listed by DIMEV, prioritizing editions with explicit manuscript apparatus.
Update work-level composition and transmission claims only when a directly checked modern source supports them.
